docs.title: Community Datasets Registry
docs.text: Community-contributed datasets for PSYCTL personality steering.

docs.title: 🗂️ Source Datasets
docs.text:
Base conversation datasets used to generate steering datasets. These contain social dialogue scenarios with situational context.

Format Example: 

{
  "narrative": "Alice is at a party...",
  "speakers": ["Friend", "Alice"],
  "dialogue": ["Want to dance?", "Sure!"]
}

Repository Language Samples License Description CaveduckAI/simplified_soda_kr Korean - - Korean version of SoDA dataset allenai/soda English ~1.5M ODC-BY Social dialogue dataset

docs.title: 🎯 Steering Datasets
docs.text:
Datasets for extracting personality steering vectors using methods like mean_diff (Mean Difference) and BiPO. Each sample contains personality-specific (positive) and neutral responses to the same situation. The extracted vectors are applied using CAA (Contrastive Activation Addition).

Format Example: 

{
  "situation": "Alice is at a party...\nFriend: Want to dance?\n",
  "char_name": "Alice",
  "positive": "Absolutely! Let's get everyone together!",
  "neutral": "Sure, I'll join you."
}

Repository Personality Language Samples Source Dataset Model License CaveduckAI/steer-personality-extroversion-ko Extroversion (외향성) Korean 100 simplified_soda_kr kimi-k2-0905 MIT CaveduckAI/steer-personality-rudeness-ko Rudeness (무례함) Korean 500 simplified_soda_kr kimi-k2-0905 MIT

1. Generate your dataset using PSYCTL

  --inventory "ipip_neo_120"

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
from psyctl.data.inventories import create_inventory

inventory = create_inventory("ipip_neo_120")
questions = inventory.get_questions()
traits = inventory.get_supported_traits()

docs.title: Big Five Domains (IPIP-NEO)
docs.text: Code Domain Description N Neuroticism Emotional instability, anxiety, moodiness E Extraversion Sociability, assertiveness, positive emotions O Openness Curiosity, imagination, preference for variety A Agreeableness Cooperation, trust, compassion C Conscientiousness Discipline, organization, goal-directed behavior

docs.title: Adding New Inventories
docs.text: See Adding Inventories for a step-by-step guide on contributing new psychological instruments.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text: This document provides solutions to common issues you may encounter when using PSYCTL.

docs.title: Understanding Padding in Batch Processing
docs.text:
When processing multiple prompts in a batch, the tokenizer pads shorter sequences to match the longest sequence in the batch. Different models use different padding directions:

  • Left padding: [PAD, PAD, Token1, Token2] (used by Gemma, GPT-2)
  • Right padding: [Token1, Token2, PAD, PAD] (used by T5, BERT)

docs.title: How PSYCTL Handles Padding
docs.text:
Good news: PSYCTL automatically handles both padding directions correctly!

The activation extraction system uses attention masks to identify the last real (non-padding) token in each sequence. This ensures correct steering vector extraction regardless of which model you use.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
1. With attention mask (automatic):
  2. Left-padded: Finds last real token correctly
  3. Right-padded: Finds last real token correctly

  4. No padding: Works as expected

  5. Without attention mask (fallback):

  6. Uses position -1 (may be incorrect for right-padded models)
  7. Logs a warning message
  8. Only occurs if internal APIs are used incorrectly

docs.title: Symptom: Warning "No attention mask set"
docs.text:
Warning message: 

WARNING: No attention mask set for layer 'model.layers[13].mlp.down_proj'.
Using position -1 (may be incorrect for right-padded models)

What it means: The activation collection system could not determine which tokens are real vs padding.

Why it matters: Without attention masks, the system falls back to using position -1, which is: - Safe for left-padded models (Gemma, GPT-2) - Potentially incorrect for right-padded models (T5, BERT)

Solution: This should not happen during normal usage. If you see this warning:

  1. Make sure you're using the official CLI or public APIs
  2. If using Python APIs directly, ensure you're not calling internal functions
  3. Report the issue with reproduction steps at: https://github.com/anthropics/psyctl/issues

docs.title: Symptom: Poor Steering Performance with T5/BERT Models
docs.text:
Problem: Steering vectors don't seem to work well with T5 or BERT-based models.

Possible causes:

  1. Incorrect layer selection: T5 and BERT have different layer naming than Llama/Gemma
  2. T5: encoder.block[N].layer[1].DenseReluDense.wo
  3. BERT: encoder.layer[N].intermediate.dense

  4. Llama/Gemma: model.layers[N].mlp.down_proj

  5. Model architecture differences: Encoder-only models (BERT) work differently than decoder-only models (Llama, Gemma)

Solution: - Use the layer inspection tools to find the correct layer paths - Start with middle layers (e.g., layer 6 out of 12) - Experiment with different layers to find what works best

docs.title: Symptom: High Memory Usage During Extraction
docs.text:
Problem: Memory usage is very high during steering vector extraction.

Causes: - Large batch size with high padding ratio - Example: Batch size 32 with prompts ranging from 10 to 500 tokens

Solution:

  1. Reduce batch size: 

    export PSYCTL_INFERENCE_BATCH_SIZE=8

  2. Sort prompts by length before batching (future feature)

  3. Use a smaller model for initial experimentation

docs.title: Supported Models
docs.text: PSYCTL has been tested with: - Gemma 2/3 series (270M, 2B, 9B, 27B) - Llama 3.1/3.2 series - Qwen series - Other decoder-only causal LMs

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
1. Encoder-only models (BERT, RoBERTa):
  2. Require different layer paths

  3. May need different steering application strategies

  4. Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART):

  5. Complex architecture with separate encoder/decoder

  6. Steering vectors may need to target specific components

  7. Very large models (70B+):

  8. May require device_map="auto" for multi-GPU
  9. Batch size needs careful tuning

docs.title: Symptom: "No JSONL files found"
docs.text:
Error: 

FileNotFoundError: No JSONL files found in directory: ./dataset/caa

Solution: 1. Check that the dataset directory exists 2. Verify the file has .jsonl extension (not .json) 3. Use ls to confirm the file is in the expected location

docs.title: Symptom: "Invalid JSON format"
docs.text:
Error: 

ValueError: Invalid JSON format at line 42

Solution: 1. Check that each line is valid JSON 2. Verify required fields: question, positive, neutral 3. Use a JSON validator to check the file

docs.title: Slow Extraction Speed
docs.text:
Causes: - Batch size too small (underutilizing GPU) - Batch size too large (memory swapping) - Model on CPU instead of GPU

Solutions:

  1. Check GPU usage: 

    nvidia-smi

  2. Optimize batch size (start with 16, adjust based on VRAM): 

    export PSYCTL_INFERENCE_BATCH_SIZE=16

  3. Ensure model loads on GPU (should see "cuda" in logs)

docs.title: Out of Memory (OOM)
docs.text:
Error: 

torch.cuda.OutOfMemoryError: CUDA out of memory

Solutions:

  1. Reduce batch size: 

    export PSYCTL_INFERENCE_BATCH_SIZE=4

  2. Use a smaller model: 

    psyctl extract.steering --model "google/gemma-3-270m-it" ...

  3. Clear GPU cache between runs: 

    import torch
torch.cuda.empty_cache()

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
If you encounter issues not covered here:

  1. Check the documentation
  2. Search existing issues
  3. Open a new issue with:
  4. Error message and full traceback
  5. Command or code that caused the error
  6. Model name and configuration
  7. System info (GPU, Python version, etc.)

docs.title: Enable Debug Logging
docs.text:
export PSYCTL_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG

This will show detailed information about: - Tokenizer padding direction - Attention mask shapes - Activation collection statistics - Layer validation results

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
psyctl --version
python -c "import torch; print(f'PyTorch: {torch.__version__}, CUDA: {torch.cuda.is_available()}')"

docs.title: Test with Minimal Example
docs.text:
Use the smallest model and smallest dataset to isolate issues:

# Quick test with tiny model
psyctl extract.steering \
  --model "google/gemma-3-270m-it" \
  --layer "model.layers[6].mlp.down_proj" \
  --dataset "path/to/small/dataset" \
  --output "./test.safetensors"
